messages.content: please edit grammar, vocabulary and spelling of the following pargraph

"As I walked down the street, I noticed a store which sold all sorts of odd things. I decided to go inside and have a look. The store was filled with curiosities, an assortment of antique figurines, old books, and exotic gadgets. I browsed for a while, taking in all of the strange objects on display. Finally, I came across an old diary. It was bound in leather and had yellowed pages. I opened it up and began to read. The diary belonged to a woman who had lived in the 1800s. She wrote about her daily life and struggles during a time when women were not deemed as important as men. I was fascinated by her words and wanted to learn more about her. I bought the diary and left the store, eager to delve deeper into this woman's life." 

Edited:
As I strolled down the street, I spotted a shop that carried an array of peculiar items. Intrigued, I decided to venture in. The establishment overflowed with an assortment of antique dolls, ancient literary works, and exotic gadgets. I leisurely perused the collection, taking in all of the oddities on full display. Eventually, I stumbled upon an aged diary bound in leather with yellowed pages. Carefully, I opened it to read. The diary belonged to a woman who had lived in the 1800s. In her entries, she recounted her daily routine and struggles in a society where women were devalued. Her words captivated me and made me curious about her life. With eagerness, I purchased the diary and left the store, keen to delve into her story.

The tectonic windows are overthrust by "Higher nappes" (i.e., Senhaja, Aknoul, and Bouhdoud nappes stack) (Andrieux, 1971; Leblanc, 1979; Frizon de Lamotte, 1985; Chalouan et al., 2008; Gimeno-Vives et al., 2020, Abbassi et al. 2020). Both are considered to be rooted in an unknown basement, probably from the Intrarif realm (Andrieux, 1971, Leblanc, 1983) or an axial zone in between the Intrarif and Mesorif sub-domains (Frizon de Lamotte et al., 2020). The frontal tectonic klippens deriving from the Intrarif-Mesorif sub-Domains had been usually reworked within a middle-late olisostrom characterized by a huge multi-kilometer thick “debris flow sheet” with a chaotic mixture of triassic redbeds, gypsum/evaporites, upper Cretaceous-Oligocene, and early-middle Miocene deposits observed in many seismic profiles (e.g. El Mourabet, 1996; Soto et al., 2017) and commonly known as the “Nappe Prérifaine” (Levy and Tilloy, 1952; Feinberg, 1986; Leblanc 1979; Kerzazi, 1994).

The tectonic windows are crossed by "Higher nappes" such as Senhaja, Aknoul, and Bouhdoud nappes stack, which override them (Andrieux, 1971; Leblanc, 1979; Frizon de Lamotte, 1985; Chalouan et al., 2008; Gimeno-Vives et al., 2020, Abbassi et al. 2020). Both are believed to have their origins in an unknown basement, likely from the Intrarif realm (Andrieux, 1971, Leblanc, 1983), or an axial zone located between the Intrarif and Mesorif sub-domains (Frizon de Lamotte et al., 2020). The frontal tectonic klippens that arise from the Intrarif-Mesorif sub-domains have been frequently reworked within a middle-late Olisostrom that features a vast multi-kilometer debris flow sheet. This sheet comprises a disorderly mixture of Triassic redbeds, evaporites, gypsum, upper Cretaceous-Oligocene, and early-middle Miocene deposits. It can be found in many seismic profiles. It is recognized as the “Nappe Prérifaine” (Levy and Tilloy, 1952; Feinberg, 1986; Leblanc 1979; Kerzazi, 1994).

The tectonic windows are cut by "Higher nappes" like Senhaja, Aknoul, and Bouhdoud nappes stack, which overthrust them (Andrieux, 1971; Leblanc, 1979; Frizon de Lamotte, 1985; Chalouan et al., 2008; Gimeno-Vives et al., 2020, Abbassi et al. 2020). Both originate from an unknown basement, most probably from the Intrarif realm (Andrieux, 1971, Leblanc, 1983), or an axial zone located between the Intrarif and Mesorif sub-domains (Frizon de Lamotte et al., 2020). The frontal tectonic klippens accumulating from the Intrarif-Mesorif sub-domains repeatedly undergo reworking within a middle-late Olisostrom, displaying a vast multi-kilometer debris flow sheet. This sheet is characterized by an incoherent mixture of Triassic redbeds, evaporites, gypsum, upper Cretaceous-Oligocene, and early-middle Miocene deposits. It is visible in numerous seismic profiles and goes by the name “Nappe Prérifaine” (Levy and Tilloy, 1952; Feinberg, 1986; Leblanc 1979; Kerzazi, 1994).

The tectonic windows represent areas of exposed pre-existing rocks surrounded by slices of one rock unit above another that have slid over each other. They are cut by "Higher nappes," including Senhaja, Aknoul, and Bouhdoud nappes stack, which overthrust them. Overthrusting involves the displacement of one rock unit over another in a horizontal or nearly horizontal direction. Both the windows and the Higher nappes have their origin in an unknown basement, which most likely derives from the Intrarif realm, a zone located beneath the mountains of North Africa. However, some researchers have suggested that the basement might be an axial zone between the Intrarif and Mesorif sub-domains.

The frontal tectonic klippens, which typically form at the edges of the windows, arises from underlying Intrarif-Mesorif sub-domains. These rocks have been repeatedly reworked within a middle-late Olisostrom, a geological formation created by gravitational instability where massive rocks, sediment and water mix, creating a flowage body of sediment material. The Olisostrom exhibits a vast multi-kilometer debris flow sheet featuring an incoherent mixture of Triassic redbeds, evaporites, gypsum, upper Cretaceous-Oligocene, and early-middle Miocene deposits. The debris flow sheet is visible in numerous seismic profiles and is known as the "Nappe Prérifaine." It represents a significant geological formation in Morocco, providing a unique perspective into the complex geological history of the region.

The tectonic windows are unique geological features that expose pre-existing rocks surrounded by slices of one rock unit above another that have slid over each other. These windows are cut by "Higher nappes," including Senhaja, Aknoul, and Bouhdoud nappes stack, which overthrust them. Overthrusting, a process involving horizontal rock displacement, is responsible for the Higher nappes climbing and overriding the windows. 

The origins of both the windows and the Higher nappes are mysterious and challenging to trace. The current consensus among geologists suggests that their basement is derived from the Intrarif realm, a geological zone between the mountains of North Africa. However, the exact location of the basement is still unknown and subject to debate. Some researchers have suggested an axial zone between the Intrarif and Mesorif sub-domains as the likely origin of the windows and Higher nappes.

The frontal tectonic klippens, typically found on the edges of the windows, arise from underlying Intrarif-Mesorif sub-domains. These klippens are frequently reworked within a middle-late Olisostrom, a geological formation characterized by massive rocks, sediment, and water mixed to create a flowage body of sediment material. The Olisostrom features a vast multi-kilometer debris flow sheet. This sheet comprises an incoherent mixture of Triassic redbeds, evaporites, gypsum, upper Cretaceous-Oligocene, and early-middle Miocene deposits, and is visible in numerous seismic profiles. The debris flow sheet is known as the "Nappe Prérifaine" and represents a significant geological formation in Morocco, providing a unique perspective into the complex geological history of the region.

The revised paragraph improved the style of writing by making the content more accessible and concise. However, the richness and accuracy of the geological terms and their definitions remained unchanged.

The External Rif Domain (Sutter, 1965 and 1980) with renowned calcareous ridges known as “Rides Prérifaines”. Both display thick sedimentary series starting from Triassic redbeds and platform carbonates up to Upper Miocene shallow water deposits (Faugère, 1978; El Mourabet, 1996; Roldan, 2014; Capella et al., 2017, Soto et al., 2017; Beelen et al.,2020, 2022; Abbassi et al.,2020). They are stacked as thrust sheets with ramp, and flat structures synchronously with the “Prerifaine nappe” sedimentation and the buoyancy and injection of salt pillows at the base of the main thrust faults and anticline cores, noticeable in seismic profiles (El Mourabet, 1996; Zizi, 1996, 2002; Sani et al., 2007; Haddaoui 2000). The External Rif sedimentary series are topped by Pliocene-quaternary formations namely in the external subsiding Saïss and Gharb basins (Ahmamou, 2002) and Finally, the external part of the foreland basin, located between the western occidental Meseta promontory and the External Prerif (s.s.), is mainly represented by the Gharb and Saïss basins (Wernli, 1988; Soto et al., 2017) with a complete sedimentary from Triassic to Quaternary continental deposits reaching thicknesses of about 1200min the Saiss Basin (e.g. Faugères, 1978; Sani et al., 2007), and 3000m in the Gharb Basin onshore and offshore deposits (Esteban et al., 1996; Capella, et al., 2017; Ahmamou, 2002).

The External Rif Domain, as described by Sutter in 1965 and 1980, is home to the famous calcareous ridges known as “Rides Prérifaines.” They showcase thick sedimentary layers extending from Triassic redbeds and platform carbonates to Upper Miocene shallow water deposits. The sedimentary layers are stacked as thrust sheets with ramp and flat structures, which coincides with the “Prérifaine nappe” sedimentation. Moreover, the thrust sheets have salt pillows injected at the base of the primary thrust faults and anticline cores, which is evident in seismic profiles. The sedimentary layers in the External Rif Domain are topped by Pliocene-Quaternary formations in the Saïss and Gharb basins that lie to the west of the region.

The foreland basin, located between the western Occidental Meseta promontory and the External Prérif, is mainly represented by the Gharb and Saïss basins. These basins demonstrate a complete sedimentary record from Triassic to Quaternary continental deposits with thicknesses reaching around 1,200m in the Saïss Basin and 3,000m in the onshore and offshore deposits of the Gharb Basin. These features are described in publications by several researchers such as Faugère in 1978, Sani in 2007, Esteban in 1996, and Capella in 2017.

The External Rif Domain, as described by Sutter in 1965 and 1980, is home to the famous calcareous ridges known as “Rides Prérifaines.” They showcase thick sedimentary layers extending from Triassic redbeds and platform carbonates to Upper Miocene shallow water deposits. The sedimentary layers are stacked as thrust sheets with ramp and flat structures, which coincides with the “Prérifaine nappe” sedimentation. Moreover, the thrust sheets have salt pillows injected at the base of the primary thrust faults and anticline cores, which is evident in seismic profiles. The sedimentary layers in the External Rif Domain are topped by Pliocene-Quaternary formations in the Saïss and Gharb basins located to the west of the region. 

The foreland basin, located between the western Occidental Meseta promontory and the External Prérif, is mainly represented by the Gharb and Saïss basins. These basins have a complete sedimentary record from Triassic to Quaternary continental deposits with thicknesses reaching around 1,200m in the Saïss Basin and 3,000m in the onshore and offshore deposits of the Gharb Basin. These features have been described in publications by several researchers, including Faugère in 1978, Sani in 2007, Esteban in 1996, and Capella in 2017.

The External Rif Domain, originally defined by Sutter in 1965 and 1980, is characterized by calcareous ridges known as the “Rides Prérifaines.” These ridges exhibit thick sedimentary sequences ranging from Triassic redbeds and platform carbonates through to Upper Miocene shallow-water deposits. The sedimentary layers are stacked as thrust sheets with ramp and flat structures, coinciding with the “Prérifaine nappe” sedimentation. Evidence of salt pillows being injected at the base of the primary thrust faults and anticline cores is visible in seismic profiles. The sedimentary layers of the External Rif Domain are capped by Pliocene-Quaternary formations, which are preserved in the Saïss and Gharb basins located west of the region.

The foreland basin, situated between the western Occidental Meseta promontory and the External Prérif, is dominated by the Gharb and Saïss basins. These basins conservatively hold a complete sedimentary record spanning Triassic to Quaternary continental deposits. Thicknesses of 1,200m in the Saïss Basin and 3,000m in both onshore and offshore deposits of the Gharb Basin have been documented. These features have been discussed in scientific literature by various authors including Faugère in 1978, Sani in 2007, Esteban in 1996, and Capella in 2017.

The External Rif Domain, originally defined by Sutter in 1965 and 1980, is characterized by calcareous ridges known as the “Rides Prérifaines” (Sutter, 1965; Sutter, 1980). These ridges exhibit thick sedimentary sequences ranging from Triassic redbeds and platform carbonates through to Upper Miocene shallow-water deposits (Faugère, 1978; El Mourabet, 1996; Roldan, 2014; Capella et al., 2017, Soto et al., 2017; Beelen et al.,2020, 2022; Abbassi et al.,2020). The sedimentary layers are stacked as thrust sheets with ramp and flat structures, coinciding with the “Prérifaine nappe” sedimentation. Evidence of salt pillows being injected at the base of the primary thrust faults and anticline cores is visible in seismic profiles (El Mourabet, 1996; Zizi, 1996, 2002; Sani et al., 2007; Haddaoui 2000). The sedimentary layers of the External Rif Domain are capped by Pliocene-Quaternary formations, which are preserved in the Saïss and Gharb basins located west of the region (Ahmamou, 2002).

The foreland basin, situated between the western Occidental Meseta promontory and the External Prérif, is dominated by the Gharb and Saïss basins (Wernli, 1988; Soto et al., 2017). These basins conservatively hold a complete sedimentary record spanning Triassic to Quaternary continental deposits. Thicknesses of 1,200m in the Saïss Basin and 3,000m in both onshore and offshore deposits of the Gharb Basin have been documented (Faugère, 1978; Sani et al., 2007, Esteban et al., 1996; Capella, et al., 2017; Ahmamou, 2002). These features have been discussed in scientific literature by various authors, including Sutter in 1965 and 1980, Faugère in 1978, Wernli in 1988, El Mourabet in 1996, Esteban in 1996, Haddaoui in 2000, Sani in 2007, Capella in 2017, Soto et al. in 2017, Roldan in 2014, and Abbassi et al. in 2020.
